behaviour.definition: The way a person acts or conducts themselves.
behaviour.example: A child's behaviour tells you what they are feeling.

communication.definition: The act of sharing information, feelings, or ideas with others.
communication.example: All behaviour is a form of communication.

section.definition: A separate part of something larger.
section.example: This section of the book is about behaviour.

desperate.definition: Feeling an extreme need for something.
desperate.example: We all behave badly when we are desperate for connection.

contact.definition: The state of being close to or touching another person.
contact.example: Children need regular contact with caring adults.

belong.definition: To feel accepted as part of a group.
belong.example: Children behave better when they feel they belong.

cradle.definition: A small bed for a baby that can rock back and forth.
cradle.example: The hand that rocks the cradle rules the world.

dismissing.definition: Not paying attention to something; treating it as unimportant.
dismissing.example: Parents should avoid dismissing their children's feelings.

infants.example: Treating infants with respect helps them grow well.

consideration.definition: Careful thought about something; also, being kind and thoughtful.
consideration.example: Treat your children with consideration and respect.

ape.example: Children will ape your behaviour, good or bad.

client.definition: A person who pays for professional services.
client.example: My client came to me for help with his relationships.

profit.definition: Money earned from a business after costs are paid.
profit.example: He ran the company for profit, not for people.

autocrat.definition: A leader who controls everything and does not listen to others.
autocrat.example: His father managed the company like an autocrat.

autocratic.definition: Controlling everything without asking for other people's opinions.
autocratic.example: His management style was autocratic, just like his father's.

charity.definition: An organization that helps people in need.
charity.example: He worked in the charity sector helping others.

sector.definition: A particular area of work or activity.
sector.example: She worked in the education sector.

department.definition: A part of a large organization that deals with specific work.
department.example: He ran his department in a controlling way.

individuals.example: We think we are individuals, but we all affect each other.

carve.definition: To cut or shape something; here, to create or establish.
carve.example: The roles we carve out are influenced by people around us.

isolation.definition: Being alone and separate from others.
isolation.example: Behaviour does not happen in isolation.

co-created.definition: Made together by more than one person.
co-created.example: A child's behaviour is co-created by family and culture.

respectful.definition: Showing care and politeness towards others.
respectful.example: Be respectful to everyone around you.

merely.example: Is your good behaviour deep, or is it merely manners?

manners.definition: Polite ways of behaving in society.
manners.example: Good manners are important but not enough on their own.

condemn.definition: To say strongly that someone or something is wrong or bad.
condemn.example: Some people are pleasant to your face but condemn you behind your back.

one-upmanship.definition: Trying to seem better or more important than someone else.

approve.definition: To think something is good or acceptable.
approve.example: Parents teach behaviour they don't approve of too.

consistently.definition: Always in the same way; without changing.
consistently.example: If you consistently show kindness, your children will too.

suit.definition: In 'follow suit': to do the same as someone else.
suit.example: If you behave kindly, your children will follow suit.

eventually.definition: In the end; after a long time.
eventually.example: Children will eventually learn to behave well.

nay.definition: An old-fashioned word meaning 'or rather' or 'even'.
nay.example: Even adults — nay, even poets — find this hard.

poets.example: Even poets struggle to put their feelings into words.

concepts.definition: Ideas or ways of thinking about something.
concepts.example: The concepts of good and bad are not always useful.

empathy.definition: The ability to understand and share another person's feelings.
empathy.example: Some people are born without the capacity to feel empathy.

wired.definition: Connected in a certain way, especially the brain.
wired.example: Having a differently wired brain does not make you bad.

relent.definition: To become less strict or harsh about something.
relent.example: I will only relent on this argument so far.

inconvenient.definition: Causing trouble or difficulty for others.
inconvenient.example: I describe difficult behaviour as inconvenient, not bad.

inappropriate.definition: Not suitable or proper for the situation.
inappropriate.example: Children act in inappropriate ways because they haven't learned better.

alternative.example: Help children find alternative ways to express their feelings.

decipher.definition: To figure out the meaning of something that is hard to understand.
decipher.example: Your job is to decipher what your child's behaviour means.

dividing.example: Rather than dividing children into good and bad bits, ask questions.
